title: How Amsterdam invented the fire department - Works in Progress Magazine
gist: Amsterdam's late-17th-century fire losses fell roughly a hundredfold after painter-inventor Jan van der Heyden and his brother Nicolaas redesigned the fire engine (suction hose, flexible 'snake' hose, pressurized air chamber) and paired it with an incentive-aligned district firefighting bureaucracy — a case study in how hardware innovation plus systems/organizational design, not capital alone, solves a public-safety problem.

themes.theme: Golden-age fire risk and the first response

insights.claim: In 17th-century Amsterdam, rising consumer wealth increased fire risk: contemporaries noted that widespread ownership of curtains, easily set alight by a stray candle, was a major ignition source that then spread to wooden furniture and walls.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows how rising consumption/wealth mechanically raises tail-risk in a pre-insurance economy — a pattern relevant to any market where asset density outpaces risk-mitigation infrastructure.
insights.quote: Curtains became far more common: easily set alight by a carelessly positioned candle, they were the source of many fires that then spread to wooden furniture and walls
insights.locator: People of all ranks owned more clothes and furnishings

insights.claim: Amsterdam's industrial base — shipyards needing stores of highly combustible pitch, tar, turpentine and hemp, plus soap-making, brewing, printing, resin-making and sugar refining — created concentrated, high-value fire hazards across the city's factories, workshops and piers.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Maps where 17th-century industrial fire risk concentrated — analogous to modern industrial-cluster catastrophe-risk underwriting.
insights.quote: highly combustible pitch, tar, turpentine, and hemp
insights.locator: Maintaining all those ships required stores

insights.claim: In the 1660s Amsterdam's city government purchased dozens of large water-pumping fire engines from Nuremberg inventor Hans Hautsch; by 1670 about 60 were deployed citywide alongside ladders, hooks, tarpaulins and more than 28,000 leather buckets, making it the largest, best-equipped firefighting system in Europe — bigger than Paris's or London's.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: An early example of a city underwriting a public-goods safety system at scale — a proto state-capacity investment with a later-measurable ROI.
insights.quote: Amsterdam’s fire-fighting system was the largest and best-equipped in Europe.

insights.claim: Firefighting duty was delegated to four Amsterdam guilds — inland sailors, peat carriers, beer carriers and grain weighers — each responsible for suppressing fires in their own city district.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows an early public-private delegation model: existing trade guilds repurposed as emergency-response infrastructure rather than a standing fire department built from scratch.
insights.quote: Members of four guilds – inland sailors, peat carriers, beer carriers, and grain weighers – were charged with fighting fires in their districts.

insights.claim: Despite Amsterdam's advanced firefighting network, a December 1669 fire at a new sugar refinery on the Laurier Canal destroyed the refinery and owner's house at an estimated cost of 195,000 guilders (roughly $20 million today), because the Hautsch engines could only spray the building's exterior and lower levels and could never reach the fire's source on the roof or deep inside.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A concrete case of 'capacity without reach' as a systemic tech-adoption failure — capital was deployed but the equipment's design was wrong, a pattern that parallels under-investment in the 'last mile' of any risk-mitigation system.
insights.quote: The streams of water sprayed only the outside of the building and the lower levels.
insights.locator: The essential problem was that fire fighters

insights.claim: Because fire insurance did not yet exist in Amsterdam (it was still roughly a century away), the sugar refinery owner's entire 1669 loss was uninsured; he died within days of the disaster.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Highlights the missing risk-transfer layer that left individual capital fully exposed to catastrophic tail events even where public fire suppression existed.
insights.quote: Fire insurance was still almost a century away, making the refinery owner’s loss total.

insights.claim: A February 1672 fire destroyed cartographer Joan Blaeu's largest printing shop — then the biggest printing operation in the world — for a total loss of 382,000 guilders, including the copper plates used for his colored Atlas maior editions that retailed for 450 guilders (roughly $45,000 today); freezing temperatures had disabled the water pumps, and Blaeu's business never recovered before his death the following year.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A second failure mode — cold-weather equipment failure — that a purely capacity-based safety system couldn't handle; infrastructure reliability requires engineering for tail conditions (freezing), not just average-case capacity.
insights.quote: even copper plates, standing in the outer corners, melted like lead
insights.locator: This great printing office, with all its contents

themes.theme: The Van der Heyden engineering fix

insights.claim: Painter and inventor Jan van der Heyden — previously known for designing and directing Amsterdam's 1,800-lamp municipal street-lighting system from 1669 — and his brother Nicolaas redesigned the fire engine with three innovations: a leather suction hose to draw water from a distance, a flexible leather 'snake' hose to aim precisely inside buildings, and an air chamber delivering continuous high-pressure flow that also resisted freezing.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A textbook case of a systems engineer (not a specialist firefighter) solving a domain problem by redesigning the interface (hose/pressure) rather than just adding more units of the old technology — a transferable innovation pattern.
insights.quote: Called a ‘snake’ (slang in Dutch), this hose could reach deeper inside buildings
insights.locator: Second, the Van der Heydens replaced

insights.claim: A January 12, 1673 fire at Amsterdam's harbor military-supply warehouses became the decisive real-world test of the new Van der Heyden engine: it arrived late and firefighters were reluctant to trust it, but it alone cleared the burning approach and enabled water to reach the main blaze, convincing the city of its superiority over the old Hautsch engines.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Illustrates the classic adoption-lag pattern for new technology — practitioners default to trusted incumbents during a crisis, and only a visible, unambiguous performance gap forces conversion.
insights.quote: A single new engine, coming late and finally reluctantly put to work on what was thought to be unavoidably lost, had preserved everything that had still been standing

insights.claim: Following the January 1673 fire, the city retired all unmodified old engines, appointed the Van der Heyden brothers supervisors of Amsterdam's fire engines and equipment, and by 1681 ordered them to supply lightweight new engines to each of the city's 60 districts along with new fire-alarm and management regulations.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Shows full technology diffusion completing within about a decade once the performance gap was demonstrated — a useful base rate for how fast a city bureaucracy can fully replace legacy safety infrastructure.
insights.quote: in 1681, the mayors ordered the Van der Heydens to produce enough engines to supply each of the city’s sixty districts

insights.claim: The Van der Heydens' new fire-response system was built on explicit market incentives: night watchmen who failed to raise the alarm paid the fines owed by the firefighters, residents who fought a fire before sounding the alarm were fined, and the first, second and third fire companies to arrive at a blaze earned monetary rewards.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A clean historical template for incentive-aligned emergency-response design (penalize delay/freelancing, reward speed) applicable to modern operational-risk system design.
insights.quote: Watchmen who failed to notify fire fighters paid those fire fighters’ fines. Householders or shopkeepers who attempted to fight a fire before sounding an alarm were also fined
insights.locator: The new system ‘was honeycombed with market incentives’

insights.claim: Fire companies were staffed by mandatory rotation rather than salary: each of the 36 men per district company had to serve a compulsory one-year term or pay a ten-guilder fine, commanded by two fire masters.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: An early conscription-plus-buyout labor model for a public-safety service, an alternative to both pure volunteering and salaried professional staffing.
insights.quote: Each man appointed had to serve a year or pay a fine of ten guilders.
insights.locator: Fire fighters were not paid salaries

insights.claim: The redesigned system cut fire losses roughly a hundredfold: from 1682 to 1687 Amsterdam suffered 41 fires with total losses of just 18,353 guilders, described in the Van der Heydens' own account as 'less than one percent of the losses from 1670 to 1681' under the old engine system.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A rare pre-modern example of a quantified, orders-of-magnitude return on a public safety-infrastructure redesign — a historical benchmark for infrastructure-investment payback arguments.
insights.quote: total losses of 18,353 guilders, or less than one percent of the losses from 1670 to 1681 under the previous system

insights.claim: Historian Lettie Stibbe Multhauf credits Jan van der Heyden's approach — designing inventions together with the operating procedures needed to make them work in practice — as 'one of the earliest instances of a method now called systems analysis.'
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: Frames Van der Heyden as a proto-systems-engineer: hardware innovation without matching organizational/process design under-delivers — a durable heuristic for evaluating any technology rollout.
insights.quote: one of the earliest instances of a method now called systems analysis

insights.claim: Amsterdam's merchant-oligarch city government — drawn from a small group of prosperous families but with broadly based commercial interests — actively sponsored 'useful' inventions and put them into practice, and citizens were noted for willingly paying high taxes for civic infrastructure because they felt they got value for it.
insights.speaker: WorksInProgMag
insights.so_what: A political-economy explanation for why this specific city could fund and sustain expensive public infrastructure: elite incentives aligned with commercial usefulness, and tax compliance followed visible returns — a governance precondition worth flagging when comparing modern infrastructure-investment jurisdictions.
insights.quote: They encouraged ‘useful’ inventions and actively sought to put these into practice
insights.locator: Although the mayors and other high city officials
